behaviors.description: An image containing explicit content is flagged and held for review, not published.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is whether the actual vision model correctly identifies explicit content in a real image. A mocked model always returns what we tell it to return, so it cannot catch real model accuracy issues. The core boundary is the real AI/ML model's behavior on actual image inputs. Without testing against the real model, we have no assurance that explicit images are actually detected in production.
behaviors.test_description: Submit a real image with explicit content through the full pipeline using the actual vision model (not mocked), verify that the real model flags it as explicit, and confirm that the post is written to the human review queue rather than the publication queue.

behaviors.description: A text post containing hate speech is flagged regardless of obfuscation techniques (e.g., letter substitution, unicode lookalikes).
behaviors.justification: The failure we care about is whether the real text model generalizes to obfuscated variants (e.g., 'h4t3_sp33ch' instead of 'hate speech'). Mocking the model to return 'hate_speech_detected' on any input doesn't test this robustness. The core boundary is the real model's ability to detect semantic equivalence across obfuscation techniques. Only the real model (or a faithful simulation of its decision boundary) can verify this behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Submit multiple text posts containing hate speech with various obfuscation techniques (letter substitution, unicode lookalikes, number/symbol replacements) using the actual text model (not mocked), and verify that all variants are flagged and held for review.

behaviors.description: A post that passes both content checks is published rather than held or silently dropped.
behaviors.justification: The real failure here is a code logic bug: the publish path isn't executed when both models return 'safe', or the post isn't actually written to the publication queue. This is a code-level bug, not a model accuracy issue. We can mock both models to reliably return 'safe' and test whether the code correctly routes the post to publication. We need Integration (not Unit) because we must verify that the post is actually persisted to a real or local database/queue, not just that the conditional logic is correct in memory.
behaviors.test_description: With both the vision and text models mocked to return 'safe' status, submit a post and verify that it is written to the publication queue/database and does not appear in the human review queue.

behaviors.description: The full moderation pipeline — image check, text check, publish decision — completes within 5 seconds of submission.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is that the pipeline exceeds the 5-second SLA under realistic conditions. Mocking both models with zero latency will never reveal slowness. The latency boundary depends on the real inference time of both AI models, network I/O, and system load. Without real (or realistically simulated) model latency, any timing test is meaningless. This is fundamentally a property of the real model execution, not the code logic.
behaviors.test_description: Run the full pipeline with both the actual vision and text models (or high-fidelity latency simulators) against representative post volumes, measure end-to-end latency from submission to publication decision, and verify that the 99th percentile latency stays under 5 seconds.

behaviors.description: When the AI vision model is unavailable, image posts are queued for retry rather than auto-approved.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is in error handling code: when the vision model call fails (connection error, timeout, service down), does the code queue the post for later retry? We don't need the real model to actually be unavailable (which would be System/Agentic). We can mock the model to raise a connection exception, and the code's retry logic will either handle it correctly or fail. The failure boundary is the code's error path, not the model's actual availability.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the vision model to raise a connection timeout exception when called. Submit an image post and verify that the post is queued for retry (stored in a retry queue with original metadata and timestamp) rather than being auto-approved or dropped.

behaviors.description: The moderation decision for a borderline image is consistent when the same image is submitted multiple times within a short window.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is that the actual vision model may return different classifications for the same borderline image due to inherent non-determinism, floating-point precision variations, or probabilistic layers in the neural network. A deterministic mock always returns the same result, hiding real inconsistency. To verify that the system maintains consistency despite the model's potential variability, we must test against the real model's actual behavior (or a faithful simulation of it) and confirm that caching or locking mechanisms prevent inconsistent verdicts.
behaviors.test_description: Using the actual vision model, submit the same borderline image 5 times within a 10-second window and verify that all submissions receive the same moderation verdict. Additionally, verify that the system uses caching or request deduplication to avoid redundant model calls.
